title: Analysis
text:
Objective: To quantify the adoption of Bayesian Adaptive Designs and Master Protocols in NSCLC.
Methodology:

Data Source: AACT (Aggregate Analysis of ClinicalTrials.gov).
Feature Engineering: Regex-based classification on brief_title and intervention_model.
QA/QC: Filtered for incomplete observations (years > 2026) and low-sample-size noise.





library(DBI); library(RPostgres); library(tidyverse); library(glue)

con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  host = Sys.getenv("AACT_DB_HOST"),
  dbname = Sys.getenv("AACT_DB_NAME"),
  user = Sys.getenv("AACT_DB_USER"),
  password = Sys.getenv("AACT_DB_PASS"),
  port = as.integer(Sys.getenv("AACT_DB_PORT")),
  sslmode = "require"
)





# Extract NCT IDs related to NSCLC
nsclc_ids <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT DISTINCT nct_id 
  FROM conditions 
  WHERE downcase_name ~* 'non-small cell lung|nsclc'
")$nct_id

# Batch pull detailed information
nct_list_string <- paste0("'", paste(nsclc_ids, collapse = "','"), "'")

#nsclc_raw <- dbGetQuery(con, paste0("
#  SELECT nct_id, brief_title, phase, start_date, completion_date, overall_status
#  FROM studies
#  WHERE nct_id IN (", nct_list_string, ")
#    AND start_date >= '2010-01-01'
#"))

# Query study information
query <- glue("
  SELECT 
    s.nct_id, 
    s.brief_title, 
    s.phase, 
    s.start_date, 
    d.intervention_model, 
    d.primary_purpose 
  FROM studies s
  LEFT JOIN designs d ON s.nct_id = d.nct_id
  WHERE s.nct_id IN ({nct_list_string}) 
  AND s.start_date >= '2010-01-01'
")

nsclc_raw <- dbGetQuery(con, query)





nsclc_interventional <- nsclc_raw %>%
  filter(!is.na(phase)) %>%
  mutate(phase_standardized = case_when(
    str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") & str_detect(phase, "PHASE3") ~ "Phase 2/3",
    str_detect(phase, "PHASE1") & str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") ~ "Phase 1/2",
    str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") ~ "Phase 2",
    str_detect(phase, "PHASE3") ~ "Phase 3",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  )) %>%
  filter(!phase_standardized %in% c("Other", "Phase 1/2"))

# Distribution
table(nsclc_interventional$phase_standardized)


  Phase 2 Phase 2/3   Phase 3 
     1229        68       419 






# Create figures directory and save the plot
# Ensure the 'figures' directory exists to store exported charts
output_dir <- "figures"
dir.create(output_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Generate the stacked bar chart for clinical trial trends
plot_trends <- nsclc_interventional %>%
  mutate(year = format(as.Date(start_date), "%Y")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(year)) %>%
  group_by(year, phase_standardized) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n, fill = phase_standardized)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Trial count",
    fill = "Clinical stage"
  )

# Export the plot to the figures directory
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "Fig. 1 nsclc_trial_trends.png"),
  plot = plot_trends,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

plot_trends




The distribution of two-thirds of NSCLC Phase clinical trials from 2010 to 2025








# Data acquisition and feature engineering
query <- glue_sql("
  SELECT 
    s.nct_id, s.brief_title, s.phase, s.start_date, s.overall_status, 
    d.intervention_model, d.primary_purpose 
  FROM studies s
  LEFT JOIN designs d ON s.nct_id = d.nct_id
  WHERE s.nct_id IN ({nsclc_ids*}) 
  AND s.start_date >= '2010-01-01'
", .con = con)

nsclc_enriched <- dbGetQuery(con, query) %>%
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(format(as.Date(start_date), "%Y")),
    is_adaptive = str_detect(tolower(brief_title), "bayesian|adaptive|seamless|sequential") | 
                  str_detect(toupper(intervention_model), "SEQUENTIAL|FACTORIAL"),
    is_master = str_detect(tolower(brief_title), "basket|umbrella|platform|master")
  )

# Data preprocessing and filtering
# Exclude data post-2026 and filter out years with insufficient sample sizes
nsclc_filtered <- nsclc_enriched %>%
  filter(year >= 2010, year <= 2026) %>% # Truncate incomplete years
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(year_total = n()) %>%           # Calculate total annual sample size
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(year_total > 5)                 # Remove years with n <= 5 (denoising)

# Trend analysis visualization
trend_data <- nsclc_filtered %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    adaptive_pct = mean(is_adaptive, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
  )

# Generate the innovation penetration rate bar chart
plot_innovation <- ggplot(trend_data, aes(x = as.factor(year), y = adaptive_pct)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Innovation Penetration Rate in NSCLC (2010-2026)", 
    y = "Percentage (%)", 
    x = "Year"
  )

# Export the plot to the figures directory
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "Fig. 2 nsclc_innovation_penetration.png"),
  plot = plot_innovation,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

plot_innovation







# Comparative success rate analysis
success_analysis <- nsclc_filtered %>%
  group_by(is_adaptive) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    success_rate = sum(overall_status == "COMPLETED", na.rm = TRUE) / n()
  )

success_analysis

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  is_adaptive total success_rate
  <lgl>       <int>        <dbl>
1 FALSE        2849        0.270
2 TRUE          423        0.170
3 NA            679        0.351





The analysis shows that Phase II clinical trials consistently accounted for the largest proportion of NSCLC studies between 2010 and 2026, indicating that early-stage efficacy evaluation remains a major focus of drug development.
The proportion of adaptive trial designs increased over time, suggesting a gradual shift from traditional fixed designs to more flexible clinical trial strategies. This trend reflects the growing use of innovative trial methodologies in NSCLC research.
A simple comparison also showed that adaptive trials had a lower completion rate than conventional trials. One possible explanation is that adaptive designs are more likely to stop early when interim results indicate limited benefit, rather than continuing to study completion.
Overall, this exploratory analysis demonstrates changing clinical trial practices in NSCLC and provides a foundation for future analyses of trial design and development efficiency.

title: Analysis
text:
1. Data Extraction and Preprocessing

library(purrr)

# Query study information
nct_list_full <- paste0("'", paste(nsclc_ids, collapse = "','"), "'")

query_studies <- glue("
  SELECT s.nct_id, s.brief_title, s.phase, s.start_date, s.completion_date, 
         d.intervention_model, d.primary_purpose
  FROM studies s 
  LEFT JOIN designs d ON s.nct_id = d.nct_id 
  WHERE s.nct_id IN ({nct_list_full}) 
  AND s.start_date >= '2010-01-01' 
")
nsclc_enriched <- dbGetQuery(con, query_studies)

# Split NCT IDs into chunks for batch processing
# Define chunk size (e.g., 500 IDs per batch)
chunk_size <- 500
nct_chunks <- split(nsclc_ids, ceiling(seq_along(nsclc_ids) / chunk_size))

# Extract outcome information via batch processing
all_outcomes_list <- list()

for (i in seq_along(nct_chunks)) {
  current_chunk <- nct_chunks[[i]]
  chunk_string <- paste0("'", paste(current_chunk, collapse = "','"), "'")
  
  query_outcomes <- glue("
    SELECT nct_id, outcome_type, title, time_frame, description
    FROM outcomes
    WHERE nct_id IN ({chunk_string})
  ")
  
  all_outcomes_list[[i]] <- dbGetQuery(con, query_outcomes)
  message(paste("Processed batch", i, "of", length(nct_chunks)))
}

Processed batch 1 of 10


Processed batch 2 of 10


Processed batch 3 of 10


Processed batch 4 of 10


Processed batch 5 of 10


Processed batch 6 of 10


Processed batch 7 of 10


Processed batch 8 of 10


Processed batch 9 of 10


Processed batch 10 of 10

# Merge results
nsclc_outcomes <- bind_rows(all_outcomes_list)

# Identify core endpoints
nsclc_outcomes_clean <- nsclc_outcomes %>%
  mutate(
    endpoint_category = case_when(
      str_detect(tolower(title), "progression-free|pfs") ~ "PFS",
      str_detect(tolower(title), "overall survival|os") ~ "OS",
      str_detect(tolower(title), "response rate|orr") ~ "ORR",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(endpoint_category != "Other")

# Link back to the original trial dataset
# Ensure the 'is_adaptive' column exists in nsclc_enriched before joining
nsclc_enriched <- nsclc_enriched %>%
  mutate(
    is_adaptive = str_detect(tolower(brief_title), "bayesian|adaptive|seamless|sequential") | 
                  str_detect(toupper(intervention_model), "SEQUENTIAL|FACTORIAL")
  )

# Perform final join
analysis_df <- nsclc_enriched %>%
  left_join(nsclc_outcomes_clean, by = "nct_id")

title: Analysis
text:
2. Trial Duration and Efficiency Analysis

# Calculate development duration (in months)
analysis_efficiency <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(
    start = as.Date(start_date),
    end = as.Date(completion_date),
    duration_months = as.numeric(end - start) / 30.44
  ) %>%
  filter(duration_months > 0 & duration_months < 120) # Remove outliers

# Compare development duration between adaptive and traditional designs
plot_duration <- ggplot(analysis_efficiency, aes(x = as.factor(is_adaptive), y = duration_months, fill = as.factor(is_adaptive))) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Clinical Trial Duration: Adaptive vs. Traditional", 
    x = "Is Adaptive Design", 
    y = "Duration (Months)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Export the plot to the figures directory
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "Fig. 3 nsclc_trial_duration_comparison.png"),
  plot = plot_duration,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

plot_duration







# Comparison after removing NA values
clean_data <- analysis_efficiency %>% filter(!is.na(is_adaptive))
wilcox.test(duration_months ~ is_adaptive, data = clean_data)


    Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction

data:  duration_months by is_adaptive
W = 1287926, p-value = 0.5643
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

title: Analysis
text:
3. Endpoint Strategy by Clinical Phase

# Standardize clinical trial phases
# Re-standardize the phase column to ensure consistency across the dataset
analysis_df <- analysis_df %>%
  mutate(
    phase_standardized = case_when(
      str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") & str_detect(phase, "PHASE3") ~ "Phase 2/3",
      str_detect(phase, "PHASE1") & str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") ~ "Phase 1/2",
      str_detect(phase, "PHASE2") ~ "Phase 2",
      str_detect(phase, "PHASE3") ~ "Phase 3",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# Calculate and display the distribution of primary endpoints
# Join trial metadata with outcomes and compute the percentage of each endpoint by phase
endpoint_strategy <- nsclc_outcomes_clean %>%
  left_join(analysis_df %>% select(nct_id, phase_standardized), by = "nct_id") %>%
  filter(!is.na(phase_standardized) & phase_standardized != "Other") %>%
  group_by(phase_standardized, endpoint_category) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  group_by(phase_standardized) %>%
  mutate(percent = count / sum(count) * 100)

Warning in left_join(., analysis_df %>% select(nct_id, phase_standardized), : Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 1 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 733 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.

knitr::kable(
  endpoint_strategy %>% arrange(phase_standardized, desc(percent)),
  caption = "Distribution of Primary Endpoints by Clinical Trial Phase",
  digits = 1
)

Key Findings

Efficiency Metrics: Statistical analysis (p>0.05) reveals no significant difference in trial duration between adaptive designs (via keyword matching) and traditional methods, suggesting that design complexity does not inherently accelerate development speed. The shorter observed durations in “NA” categories are primarily attributed to small-scale or non-interventional studies.
Strategic Endpoint Hierarchy: A distinct hierarchy is evident in clinical development: early-phase trials emphasize safety and rapid signals (ORR/PFS), whereas Phase III trials shift toward Overall Survival (OS) as the definitive measure of utility. The prevalence of OS in Phase I/II trials suggests a cautious, long-term monitoring strategy for molecularly targeted sub-cohorts.

title: index
text:
A reproducible R-based project analyzing trends in innovative clinical trial designs for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) from 2010 to 2026.

This project investigates how modern clinical trial methodologies—including Bayesian adaptive designs and master protocols—have evolved in NSCLC clinical research. By integrating data from the ClinicalTrials.gov AACT database with public genomic resources (TCGA), the project explores both methodological trends and their biological rationale.




Quantify the adoption of Bayesian adaptive designs and master protocols over time.
Compare innovative and conventional trial designs using clinical trial outcomes.
Integrate TCGA biomarker data (e.g., EGFR, KRAS) to illustrate the biological rationale for precision-oriented clinical trial designs.
Maintain a fully reproducible statistical analysis workflow using R and Quarto.





ClinicalTrials.gov AACT: Clinical trial registry data.
TCGA / GDC: Public genomic and biomarker datasets.
